axioms.name: Brooks' Law
axioms.formalDefinition: Adding human resources to a late software project makes it later, due to increased communication overhead that grows as n(n-1)/2.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Communication channels = n(n-1)/2
boundaryConditions: Does not apply to perfectly partitionable tasks
Less relevant for asynchronous work
canonicalCitations: Brooks, F. (1975). The Mythical Man-Month.

axioms.name: Gresham's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: When two forms of commodity money are in circulation and accepted by law as having equivalent face value, the more valuable commodity (good money) will be hoarded and the less valuable (bad money) will circulate, driving good money out of circulation.
boundaryConditions: Requires legal tender laws enforcing parity
Does not apply when exchange rates float freely
canonicalCitations: Gresham, T. (1558). Letter to Queen Elizabeth I.
Mundell, R. (1998). Uses and Abuses of Gresham's Law.

axioms.name: Coase's Theory of the Firm
axioms.formalDefinition: Firms exist because they reduce transaction costs that would otherwise be incurred through market-based exchanges. The boundary of the firm is determined by the point at which internal coordination costs equal external transaction costs.
boundaryConditions: Assumes transaction costs are the primary determinant of firm boundaries
Less explanatory for firms driven by regulatory capture or network effects
canonicalCitations: Coase, R. (1937). The Nature of the Firm. Economica.

axioms.name: Alignment Tax
axioms.formalDefinition: The overhead cost of ensuring that multiple agents or teams work toward a coherent shared objective rather than optimizing locally. As organizational complexity increases, the fraction of total effort consumed by coordination grows nonlinearly.
boundaryConditions: Negligible for very small teams with shared context
Can be mitigated by clear interfaces and modular architecture
canonicalCitations: Komoroske, A. (2020). Alignment Tax Concept.
Galbraith, J. (1973). Designing Complex Organizations.

axioms.name: Metcalfe's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: The value of a telecommunications network is proportional to the square of the number of connected users, because each new user creates potential connections with all existing users.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: V = k * n^2
boundaryConditions: Assumes all connections are equally valuable
Overestimates value for large networks where most connections are unused
Reed's Law may apply for group-forming networks
canonicalCitations: Metcalfe, R. (1980). Ethernet concept.
Gilder, G. (1993). Metcalfe's Law and Legacy. Forbes.

axioms.name: Pareto Principle
axioms.formalDefinition: In many systems, roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. This power-law distribution appears across economics, software engineering, quality management, and natural phenomena.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(X > x) ~ x^(-alpha), alpha ~ 1.16 for 80/20
boundaryConditions: The exact ratio varies; 80/20 is an approximation
Does not apply to normally distributed phenomena
canonicalCitations: Pareto, V. (1896). Cours d'economie politique.
Juran, J. (1951). Quality Control Handbook.

axioms.name: Amdahl's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: The maximum speedup of a system achievable through parallelization is limited by the fraction of the workload that must remain sequential. Even infinite parallel resources cannot overcome the serial bottleneck.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Speedup = 1 / (s + (1-s)/p), where s = serial fraction, p = parallel processors
boundaryConditions: Assumes the serial fraction is fixed
Gustafson's Law provides an alternative when problem size scales with resources
canonicalCitations: Amdahl, G. (1967). Validity of the Single Processor Approach.

axioms.name: Elasticity of Demand
axioms.formalDefinition: The percentage change in quantity demanded of a good in response to a one percent change in its price. Elastic goods (|E|>1) see proportionally larger demand changes; inelastic goods (|E|<1) see proportionally smaller demand changes.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: E_d = (dQ/Q) / (dP/P) = (dQ/dP) * (P/Q)
boundaryConditions: Elasticity varies along the demand curve
Short-run and long-run elasticities differ significantly
Assumes ceteris paribus
canonicalCitations: Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics.

axioms.name: Marginal Analysis
axioms.formalDefinition: Optimal decisions are made at the margin: an activity should continue as long as marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost. The relevant comparison is always the next unit, not the average or total.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Optimize where MB = MC (marginal benefit equals marginal cost)
boundaryConditions: Requires continuous decision variables
Fails when decisions are lumpy or indivisible
Requires accurate measurement of marginal quantities
canonicalCitations: Jevons, W.S. (1871). The Theory of Political Economy.
Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics.

axioms.name: Goodhart's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. Agents will optimize the metric itself rather than the underlying phenomenon it was intended to track, causing the correlation between metric and goal to break down.
boundaryConditions: Less problematic when the measure directly captures the goal
Mitigated by using multiple orthogonal metrics
canonicalCitations: Goodhart, C. (1975). Monetary Relationships.
Strathern, M. (1997). Improving Ratings: Audit in the British University System.

axioms.name: Conway's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: Organizations that design systems are constrained to produce designs that mirror the communication structures of those organizations. System architecture reflects organizational architecture.
boundaryConditions: Can be deliberately leveraged via 'inverse Conway maneuver'
Less deterministic in small organizations with fluid communication
canonicalCitations: Conway, M. (1968). How Do Committees Invent? Datamation.

axioms.name: Theory of Constraints
axioms.formalDefinition: System throughput is determined by the single tightest constraint (bottleneck). Improvements to non-constraint resources do not improve overall system performance. The five focusing steps: identify, exploit, subordinate, elevate, and repeat.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: System throughput <= min(throughput of each stage)
boundaryConditions: Assumes a single dominant constraint at any time
Less applicable to highly parallel systems without serial dependencies
canonicalCitations: Goldratt, E. (1984). The Goal.

axioms.name: Diminishing Marginal Returns
axioms.formalDefinition: As one input factor is increased while others are held constant, the marginal output of that factor eventually decreases. Each additional unit of input produces less additional output than the previous unit.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: d^2Y/dX^2 < 0 (concave production function)
boundaryConditions: Applies only when other inputs are held fixed
May not apply in early stages where increasing returns dominate
Network effects can reverse diminishing returns
canonicalCitations: Turgot, A.R.J. (1767). Observations on Taxation.
Ricardo, D. (1817). Principles of Political Economy.

axioms.name: Economies of Scale
axioms.formalDefinition: Average cost per unit decreases as the quantity produced increases, due to fixed cost spreading, specialization, and bulk purchasing advantages. Larger producers enjoy structural cost advantages.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: AC(Q) = FC/Q + VC(Q)/Q, where dAC/dQ < 0
boundaryConditions: Eventually reversed by diseconomies of scale
Less relevant for purely digital goods with near-zero marginal cost
canonicalCitations: Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics.
Chandler, A. (1990). Scale and Scope.

axioms.name: Diseconomies of Scale
axioms.formalDefinition: Beyond a certain size, average costs increase with additional output due to coordination complexity, bureaucratic overhead, communication breakdowns, and loss of organizational agility.
boundaryConditions: Threshold varies dramatically by industry and organizational design
Can be mitigated by modular organizational structures
canonicalCitations: Williamson, O. (1967). Hierarchical Control and Optimum Firm Size.
Coase, R. (1937). The Nature of the Firm.

axioms.name: Principal-Agent Problem
axioms.formalDefinition: When one party (the agent) makes decisions on behalf of another (the principal), incentive misalignment and information asymmetry lead the agent to act in their own interest rather than the principal's. The core challenge is designing contracts that align agent behavior with principal interests.
boundaryConditions: Mitigated when agent's interests naturally align with principal's
Less relevant when monitoring costs are very low
canonicalCitations: Jensen, M. & Meckling, W. (1976). Theory of the Firm.
Ross, S. (1973). The Economic Theory of Agency.

axioms.name: Comparative Advantage
axioms.formalDefinition: An entity benefits from specializing in and trading goods or services for which it has the lowest opportunity cost, even if it holds absolute advantage in all areas. Trade based on comparative advantage increases total output.
boundaryConditions: Assumes free and low-cost trade
May not hold with significant externalities or strategic industries
Dynamic comparative advantage can shift over time
canonicalCitations: Ricardo, D. (1817). On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation.

axioms.name: Opportunity Cost
axioms.formalDefinition: The true cost of any choice is the value of the best alternative foregone. Every resource allocation implies a trade-off, and rational decision-making requires comparing options against their next-best alternatives, not against zero.
boundaryConditions: Difficult to calculate when alternatives are uncertain or numerous
Cognitive limitations prevent full enumeration of alternatives
canonicalCitations: Bastiat, F. (1850). That Which Is Seen and That Which Is Not Seen.
Buchanan, J. (1969). Cost and Choice.

axioms.name: Framing Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: Decisions are influenced by how information is presented (framed) rather than by the information itself. Equivalent options described as gains vs. losses, or with different reference points, produce systematically different choices.
boundaryConditions: Effect is weaker for experts with deep domain knowledge
Diminishes when stakes are very high and deliberation time is long
canonicalCitations: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1981). The Framing of Decisions and the Psychology of Choice. Science.

axioms.name: Cognitive Load Theory
axioms.formalDefinition: Working memory has severe capacity limits (approximately 7 plus or minus 2 items). When the intrinsic complexity of material plus the extraneous load from poor design exceeds working memory capacity, learning and performance degrade sharply.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Total load = Intrinsic load + Extraneous load + Germane load <= Working memory capacity
boundaryConditions: Experts have chunked knowledge that reduces intrinsic load
Does not apply to highly automated/proceduralized tasks
canonicalCitations: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive Load During Problem Solving. Cognitive Science.
Miller, G.A. (1956). The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two.

axioms.name: Attention Economics
axioms.formalDefinition: In an information-rich world, the wealth of information creates a poverty of attention. Attention becomes the scarce resource that constrains information processing, making the ability to filter and prioritize more valuable than the ability to produce information.
boundaryConditions: Less relevant in information-scarce environments
Assumes attention cannot be significantly expanded through tools or delegation
canonicalCitations: Simon, H. (1971). Designing Organizations for an Information-Rich World.
Davenport, T. & Beck, J. (2001). The Attention Economy.

axioms.name: Bounded Rationality
axioms.formalDefinition: Human decision-makers lack the information, cognitive capacity, and time to optimize. Instead, they satisfice: searching for solutions that meet an acceptable threshold rather than the global optimum. Organizations amplify and constrain individual bounded rationality through routines and structures.
boundaryConditions: In simple, well-defined domains with few options, humans can approach full rationality
Computational aids can extend the bounds
canonicalCitations: Simon, H. (1955). A Behavioral Model of Rational Choice. Quarterly Journal of Economics.
Simon, H. (1972). Theories of Bounded Rationality.

axioms.name: Confirmation Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms or supports one's prior beliefs or hypotheses. Disconfirming evidence is unconsciously discounted or ignored, creating a self-reinforcing belief loop.
boundaryConditions: Can be partially mitigated by structured adversarial review processes
Effect is stronger for emotionally charged beliefs
canonicalCitations: Wason, P. (1960). On the failure to eliminate hypotheses in a conceptual task.
Nickerson, R. (1998). Confirmation Bias: A Ubiquitous Phenomenon in Many Guises.

axioms.name: Anchoring Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: Initial exposure to a number or value (the anchor) disproportionately influences subsequent judgments, even when the anchor is arbitrary or irrelevant. Adjustment from the anchor is typically insufficient, biasing estimates toward the anchor.
boundaryConditions: Expertise reduces but does not eliminate anchoring
Multiple competing anchors can partially offset the effect
canonicalCitations: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1974). Judgment Under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases. Science.

axioms.name: Status Quo Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: People exhibit a disproportionate preference for the current state of affairs. The potential losses from changing are psychologically weighted more heavily than the potential gains, creating inertia even when alternatives are objectively superior.
boundaryConditions: Overcome when the current state becomes clearly untenable
Weaker for those with less investment in the current state
canonicalCitations: Samuelson, W. & Zeckhauser, R. (1988). Status Quo Bias in Decision Making. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty.

axioms.name: Nash Equilibrium
axioms.formalDefinition: A state in which no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy, given the strategies of all other players. A Nash equilibrium may be suboptimal for the group while being individually rational for each player.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: For all players i: u_i(s_i*, s_{-i}*) >= u_i(s_i, s_{-i}*) for all s_i
boundaryConditions: Multiple Nash equilibria may exist
Assumes players know others' strategies
Does not guarantee Pareto optimality
canonicalCitations: Nash, J. (1950). Equilibrium Points in N-Person Games. Proceedings of the NAS.
Nash, J. (1951). Non-Cooperative Games. Annals of Mathematics.

axioms.name: Prisoner's Dilemma
axioms.formalDefinition: A game where two rational agents acting in their individual self-interest produce an outcome that is worse for both than if they had cooperated. The dominant strategy (defect) leads to a Pareto-inferior equilibrium, illustrating the tension between individual and collective rationality.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: T > R > P > S and 2R > T + S (temptation > reward > punishment > sucker)
boundaryConditions: Cooperation can emerge in iterated games (Axelrod's tournaments)
Enforceable contracts or third-party enforcement can resolve the dilemma
canonicalCitations: Tucker, A. (1950). The Prisoner's Dilemma.
Axelrod, R. (1984). The Evolution of Cooperation.

axioms.name: Schelling Focal Point
axioms.formalDefinition: In coordination games with multiple equilibria, players tend to converge on solutions that are culturally, psychologically, or contextually salient, even without communication. The focal point serves as a natural coordination mechanism arising from shared context.
boundaryConditions: Requires shared cultural or contextual knowledge
Less effective when players come from very different backgrounds
canonicalCitations: Schelling, T. (1960). The Strategy of Conflict.

axioms.name: Chicken Game
axioms.formalDefinition: A game where two players drive toward each other; the first to swerve loses face but survives, while if neither swerves both suffer the worst outcome. Unlike the Prisoner's Dilemma, mutual defection is the worst outcome for both players, creating incentives for brinkmanship and commitment devices.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: T > R > S > P (temptation > reward > sucker > punishment, where punishment is mutual destruction)
boundaryConditions: Requires credible threat of mutual destruction
Communication and commitment devices can shift outcomes
canonicalCitations: Russell, B. (1959). Common Sense and Nuclear Warfare.
Rapoport, A. (1966). Two-Person Game Theory.

axioms.name: Tragedy of the Commons
axioms.formalDefinition: When a shared resource is non-excludable but rivalrous, individual rational exploitation leads to collective overuse and depletion. Each user captures the full benefit of consumption but bears only a fraction of the cost of depletion.
boundaryConditions: Can be resolved through privatization, regulation, or community governance (Ostrom)
Does not apply to non-rivalrous goods
canonicalCitations: Hardin, G. (1968). The Tragedy of the Commons. Science.
Ostrom, E. (1990). Governing the Commons.

axioms.name: Feedback Loops
axioms.formalDefinition: Circular causal chains where the output of a system feeds back as input. Reinforcing (positive) loops amplify change and drive exponential growth or collapse. Balancing (negative) loops counteract change and drive the system toward equilibrium or a goal.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Reinforcing: dx/dt = kx (exponential). Balancing: dx/dt = k(goal - x)
boundaryConditions: Reinforcing loops always eventually encounter a balancing constraint
Delays in feedback can cause oscillation and overshoot
canonicalCitations: Forrester, J. (1961). Industrial Dynamics.
Meadows, D. (2008). Thinking in Systems.

axioms.name: Limits to Growth
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where a reinforcing growth process encounters a balancing constraint that slows and eventually halts growth. The constraint may be resource depletion, market saturation, or organizational capacity. Early growth misleadingly suggests unlimited potential.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Logistic growth: dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K), where K = carrying capacity
boundaryConditions: The limiting factor can sometimes be removed or expanded
Multiple sequential limits may exist
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Meadows, D. et al. (1972). The Limits to Growth.

axioms.name: Shifting the Burden
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where a symptomatic solution reduces immediate problem visibility, undermining motivation to pursue the fundamental solution. Over time, the fundamental solution atrophies while dependence on the symptomatic fix increases.
boundaryConditions: Requires that the symptomatic fix actually reduces problem visibility
Does not apply when the symptomatic and fundamental solutions are the same
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Braun, W. (2002). The System Archetypes.

axioms.name: Leverage Points
axioms.formalDefinition: Points within a complex system where a small shift can produce large changes in system behavior. Meadows identified a hierarchy of leverage points, from parameters and buffers (low leverage) through feedback loops and rules to paradigms and transcendence (high leverage).
boundaryConditions: High-leverage interventions are often counterintuitive
Identification requires deep understanding of system structure
canonicalCitations: Meadows, D. (1999). Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System.
Meadows, D. (2008). Thinking in Systems.

axioms.name: Causal Loop Diagrams
axioms.formalDefinition: A visual mapping technique that represents variables and their causal relationships as directed graphs with polarity indicators. Same-direction links (+) and opposite-direction links (-) combine to form reinforcing loops (even number of negative links) and balancing loops (odd number of negative links).
boundaryConditions: Cannot capture nonlinear relationships accurately
Omit time delays unless explicitly annotated
Qualitative only; do not represent magnitudes
canonicalCitations: Sterman, J. (2000). Business Dynamics: Systems Thinking and Modeling for a Complex World.
Richardson, G. (1986). Problems with Causal-Loop Diagrams.

axioms.name: Path Dependence
axioms.formalDefinition: Outcomes are significantly shaped by historical sequence and prior decisions, even when those initial conditions are no longer optimal. Early choices constrain future possibilities, creating lock-in that may persist long after the original reasons have become irrelevant.
boundaryConditions: External shocks can break path dependence
Applies most strongly when increasing returns reinforce early choices
canonicalCitations: Arthur, W.B. (1989). Competing Technologies, Increasing Returns, and Lock-In.
David, P. (1985). Clio and the Economics of QWERTY.

axioms.name: Tipping Points
axioms.formalDefinition: Critical thresholds at which a system transitions from one qualitative state to another. Small perturbations near the tipping point can trigger large, often irreversible shifts in system behavior. The system exhibits nonlinear dynamics with discontinuous jumps.
boundaryConditions: Not all systems have sharp tipping points; some transition gradually
Identifying tipping points in advance is extremely difficult
canonicalCitations: Gladwell, M. (2000). The Tipping Point.
Scheffer, M. (2009). Critical Transitions in Nature and Society.

axioms.name: Network Effects
axioms.formalDefinition: The value of a product or service to a user increases as more users adopt it. Direct network effects arise when users directly benefit from other users (e.g., phone network). Indirect network effects arise through complementary goods (e.g., more users attract more developers).
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: V_user ~ f(n), where f is increasing and often convex initially
boundaryConditions: Network effects can turn negative at extreme scale (congestion, spam)
Require a minimum viable network to activate
canonicalCitations: Katz, M. & Shapiro, C. (1985). Network Externalities, Competition, and Compatibility. AER.
Liebowitz, S. & Margolis, S. (1994). Network Externality: An Uncommon Tragedy.

axioms.name: Disruption Theory
axioms.formalDefinition: Established firms fail when new entrants introduce simpler, cheaper products that initially serve low-end or non-consumers, then improve over time to capture mainstream markets. Incumbents rationally ignore the low-end threat because their most profitable customers demand continued investment in sustaining innovation.
boundaryConditions: Does not apply when the incumbent can adopt the disruptive technology
Less relevant in regulated markets with high barriers to entry
canonicalCitations: Christensen, C. (1997). The Innovator's Dilemma.

axioms.name: Modularity Theory
axioms.formalDefinition: Complex systems can be decomposed into semi-independent modules with well-defined interfaces. Modular architectures enable independent innovation within components, while integral architectures optimize cross-component interactions at the cost of flexibility.
boundaryConditions: Defining clean interfaces has a real cost
Some systems have inherently integral architectures that resist modularization
canonicalCitations: Baldwin, C. & Clark, K. (2000). Design Rules: The Power of Modularity.
Christensen, C. et al. (2004). Seeing What's Next.

axioms.name: Oscillation Archetype
axioms.formalDefinition: Systems with delays in their feedback loops tend to overshoot and undershoot targets, creating oscillatory behavior. The delay between action and perceived effect causes decision-makers to over-correct, amplifying the oscillation rather than dampening it.
boundaryConditions: Oscillation dampens when feedback delays are short
External stabilizers or buffers can reduce oscillation amplitude
canonicalCitations: Sterman, J. (1989). Modeling Managerial Behavior: Misperceptions of Feedback.
Forrester, J. (1961). Industrial Dynamics.

axioms.name: Accumulation (Stocks and Flows)
axioms.formalDefinition: System behavior is driven by stocks (accumulations) that change only through inflows and outflows. Stocks create delays, provide buffers, and decouple rates of flow. Most people underestimate the time required for stocks to change, leading to poor dynamic reasoning.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Stock(t) = Stock(t0) + integral(inflow - outflow, t0, t)
boundaryConditions: Applies only to conserved quantities
Non-physical stocks (reputation, trust) may not follow strict conservation
canonicalCitations: Forrester, J. (1961). Industrial Dynamics.
Sterman, J. (2000). Business Dynamics.

axioms.name: Dunbar's Number
axioms.formalDefinition: The cognitive limit on the number of stable social relationships an individual can maintain, estimated at approximately 150. Beyond this threshold, groups require formal hierarchies, rules, and norms to maintain cohesion, as informal trust-based coordination breaks down.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Approximate limit ~ 150 (with nested layers at ~5, ~15, ~50)
boundaryConditions: Digital tools may extend but not eliminate the limit
The number varies by individual and type of relationship
canonicalCitations: Dunbar, R. (1992). Neocortex Size as a Constraint on Group Size in Primates. Journal of Human Evolution.

axioms.name: Parkinson's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: Work expands to fill the time available for its completion. Bureaucracies grow at a predictable rate (5-7% per year) regardless of the amount of work to be done, driven by subordinate multiplication and make-work dynamics.
boundaryConditions: Does not apply under severe resource constraints
Tight deadlines with accountability can counteract the effect
canonicalCitations: Parkinson, C.N. (1955). Parkinson's Law. The Economist.
Parkinson, C.N. (1958). Parkinson's Law: The Pursuit of Progress.

axioms.name: Innovation S-Curve
axioms.formalDefinition: Technologies follow a characteristic S-shaped adoption and performance trajectory: slow initial progress during exploration, rapid improvement during growth, and eventual plateau as fundamental limits are approached. New S-curves often begin before the current one plateaus.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Performance(t) = L / (1 + e^(-k(t-t0))), logistic curve
boundaryConditions: Not all technologies reach the rapid improvement phase
The plateau level is often underestimated during the early phase
canonicalCitations: Foster, R. (1986). Innovation: The Attacker's Advantage.
Rogers, E. (1962). Diffusion of Innovations.

axioms.name: Crossing the Chasm
axioms.formalDefinition: Technology adoption follows a bell curve of adopter categories (innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, laggards), with a critical gap ('chasm') between early adopters and the early majority. Early adopters are visionaries who tolerate imperfection; the early majority are pragmatists who require proven, complete solutions.
boundaryConditions: Less relevant for technologies with network effects that create natural pull
The chasm varies in width by market segment
canonicalCitations: Moore, G. (1991). Crossing the Chasm.

axioms.name: Jevons Paradox
axioms.formalDefinition: When technological progress increases the efficiency of resource use, total resource consumption may increase rather than decrease, because the lower effective cost stimulates increased demand that more than offsets the efficiency gain.
boundaryConditions: Only occurs when demand elasticity exceeds unity
Does not apply when total demand is fixed or capped
canonicalCitations: Jevons, W.S. (1865). The Coal Question.
Alcott, B. (2005). Jevons' Paradox. Ecological Economics.

axioms.name: Induced Demand
axioms.formalDefinition: When the effective cost of a resource or service decreases (through capacity expansion, price reduction, or efficiency improvement), new demand materializes that was previously latent. The new use cases were not merely suppressed but genuinely did not exist at the higher cost.
boundaryConditions: Requires latent demand to exist at lower price points
Does not apply when the resource has no alternative uses
canonicalCitations: Downs, A. (1962). The Law of Peak-Hour Expressway Congestion. Traffic Quarterly.
Goodwin, P. (1996). Empirical Evidence on Induced Traffic.

axioms.name: Winner-Take-All
axioms.formalDefinition: Markets where small differences in quality, luck, or timing lead to enormous differences in reward. Network effects, economies of scale, and switching costs create positive feedback loops that concentrate market share, often resulting in one dominant player capturing disproportionate value.
boundaryConditions: Requires strong increasing returns or network effects
Regulation or interoperability mandates can prevent full concentration
canonicalCitations: Frank, R. & Cook, P. (1995). The Winner-Take-All Society.
Arthur, W.B. (1996). Increasing Returns and the New World of Business. HBR.

axioms.name: Veblen Good
axioms.formalDefinition: A good for which demand increases as the price increases, because the high price itself confers status or signals quality. This violates the standard law of demand and creates an upward-sloping demand curve in the prestige segment.
boundaryConditions: Only applies when conspicuous consumption or status signaling is the primary motivation
The Veblen effect breaks down if the good becomes widely accessible
canonicalCitations: Veblen, T. (1899). The Theory of the Leisure Class.
Leibenstein, H. (1950). Bandwagon, Snob, and Veblen Effects.

axioms.name: Asymmetric Information
axioms.formalDefinition: When one party in a transaction has materially more or better information than the other, markets fail to achieve efficient outcomes. Adverse selection (hidden information before the transaction) and moral hazard (hidden action after the transaction) are the primary consequences.
boundaryConditions: Mitigated by signaling, screening, and reputation mechanisms
Less relevant when information can be cheaply verified
canonicalCitations: Akerlof, G. (1970). The Market for Lemons. QJE.
Spence, M. (1973). Job Market Signaling. QJE.

axioms.name: Moral Hazard
axioms.formalDefinition: When an entity is insulated from the consequences of their decisions (by insurance, bailouts, or delegation), they take greater risks than they would if fully exposed to the downside. The separation of risk-bearing from decision-making systematically distorts behavior.
boundaryConditions: Mitigated by monitoring, co-insurance, and skin-in-the-game requirements
Less relevant when the agent's reputation is at stake
canonicalCitations: Arrow, K. (1963). Uncertainty and the Welfare Economics of Medical Care. AER.
Pauly, M. (1968). The Economics of Moral Hazard.

axioms.name: Regression to the Mean
axioms.formalDefinition: Extreme observations on any variable tend to be followed by observations closer to the population mean, purely as a statistical phenomenon. This is not a causal force but a mathematical consequence of imperfect correlation, yet it is routinely misinterpreted as a causal effect.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: E[Y|X=x] = mu_Y + rho * sigma_Y/sigma_X * (x - mu_X), where |rho| < 1
boundaryConditions: Does not apply when the variable is deterministic
The phenomenon disappears when correlation approaches 1
canonicalCitations: Galton, F. (1886). Regression Towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature.
Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.

axioms.name: Survivorship Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: The logical error of concentrating on entities that survived a selection process and overlooking those that did not, leading to false conclusions about what caused success. The visible survivors are a non-representative sample of the original population.
boundaryConditions: Less problematic when the selection process is well-understood
Can be corrected by deliberately studying failures
canonicalCitations: Wald, A. (1943). Statistical Research Group memorandum on aircraft armor.
Brown, S.J. et al. (1992). Survivorship Bias in Performance Studies. Review of Financial Studies.

axioms.name: Planning Fallacy
axioms.formalDefinition: The systematic tendency to underestimate the time, cost, and risk of planned actions while overestimating their benefits. People plan based on best-case scenarios (inside view) rather than base rates from similar past projects (outside view).
boundaryConditions: Can be mitigated by reference class forecasting
Experienced planners in repetitive domains show less bias
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Intuitive Prediction: Biases and Corrective Procedures.
Buehler, R. et al. (1994). Exploring the Planning Fallacy.

axioms.name: Curse of Knowledge
axioms.formalDefinition: Once a person knows something, they find it extremely difficult to think about problems from the perspective of someone who lacks that knowledge. Experts systematically overestimate how obvious or accessible their knowledge is to non-experts.
boundaryConditions: Partially mitigated by structured perspective-taking exercises
Stronger for tacit knowledge than explicit knowledge
canonicalCitations: Camerer, C. et al. (1989). The Curse of Knowledge in Economic Settings.
Heath, C. & Heath, D. (2007). Made to Stick.

axioms.name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to continue investing in a course of action because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) that cannot be recovered, rather than making decisions based solely on prospective costs and benefits.
boundaryConditions: Sunk costs can be rationally relevant when they signal commitment to others
Less prevalent in organizations with strong kill-criteria for projects
canonicalCitations: Arkes, H. & Blumer, C. (1985). The Psychology of Sunk Cost.
Thaler, R. (1980). Toward a Positive Theory of Consumer Choice.

axioms.name: Supply and Demand Equilibrium
axioms.formalDefinition: Market prices adjust to balance the quantity supplied with the quantity demanded. At the equilibrium price, there is no tendency for change. Shifts in supply or demand curves produce predictable changes in equilibrium price and quantity.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Q_d(P*) = Q_s(P*), where P* is the equilibrium price
boundaryConditions: Assumes competitive markets with flexible prices
Does not apply to markets with price controls, rationing, or monopoly power
canonicalCitations: Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics.
Walras, L. (1874). Elements of Pure Economics.

axioms.name: Creative Destruction
axioms.formalDefinition: The process by which new innovations render existing products, services, and business models obsolete. Economic progress requires the continuous destruction of old structures, and attempts to prevent this destruction reduce dynamism and long-term growth.
boundaryConditions: The transition period creates real displacement costs that merit attention
Some destruction is not creative but merely destructive
canonicalCitations: Schumpeter, J. (1942). Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy.

axioms.name: Complementary Goods
axioms.formalDefinition: Goods where increased consumption of one increases demand for the other. Strategic firms commoditize their complements to capture more value: when one layer of a value stack becomes cheap, the adjacent layer becomes more valuable.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Cross-elasticity E_xy < 0 (negative cross-price elasticity)
boundaryConditions: Complement relationships can shift as technology evolves
Does not apply when goods are substitutes
canonicalCitations: Brandenburger, A. & Nalebuff, B. (1996). Co-opetition.
Shapiro, C. & Varian, H. (1998). Information Rules.

axioms.name: Two-Sided Market
axioms.formalDefinition: A platform that serves two distinct user groups who provide each other with network benefits. The platform must solve the chicken-and-egg problem of attracting both sides simultaneously, and pricing must account for cross-side externalities.
boundaryConditions: Requires genuine cross-side externalities, not just multi-product bundling
May tip to monopoly or sustain competition depending on multi-homing costs
canonicalCitations: Rochet, J.-C. & Tirole, J. (2003). Platform Competition in Two-Sided Markets.
Evans, D. & Schmalensee, R. (2007). Catalyst Code.

axioms.name: Switching Costs
axioms.formalDefinition: The costs a consumer incurs when changing from one product or supplier to another. These include financial costs, learning costs, data migration costs, relationship costs, and psychological costs. High switching costs create lock-in and reduce competitive pressure.
boundaryConditions: Switching costs can be reduced by standardization and interoperability
Regulatory action can mandate portability to reduce switching costs
canonicalCitations: Klemperer, P. (1987). Markets with Consumer Switching Costs. QJE.
Shapiro, C. & Varian, H. (1998). Information Rules.

axioms.name: Giffen Good
axioms.formalDefinition: A theoretical inferior good for which the income effect of a price increase outweighs the substitution effect, causing quantity demanded to rise as price rises. Unlike Veblen goods, the mechanism is not status signaling but the squeezing of the consumer's budget toward the cheapest essential option.
boundaryConditions: Extremely rare in practice; requires the good to constitute a large share of the consumer's budget
Only applies to inferior goods with few substitutes
canonicalCitations: Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics (attributed to Giffen).
Jensen, R. & Miller, N. (2008). Giffen Behavior and Subsistence Consumption. AER.

axioms.name: Wardley Mapping
axioms.formalDefinition: A strategic visualization technique that maps components of a value chain on two axes: visibility to the user (vertical) and evolutionary stage (horizontal, from genesis through custom-built and product to commodity). Components at different stages require different management approaches.
boundaryConditions: Placement on the evolution axis is subjective and debatable
The map is a simplification that may omit important interdependencies
canonicalCitations: Wardley, S. (2016). Wardley Maps: Topographical Intelligence in Business.

axioms.name: Hick's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: Decision time increases logarithmically with the number of equally probable alternatives. More choices increase cognitive load and decision latency, eventually leading to decision paralysis when options exceed processing capacity.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: RT = a + b * log2(n), where n = number of alternatives
boundaryConditions: Does not apply when options are not equally probable
Expertise and familiarity reduce the effect through chunking
canonicalCitations: Hick, W.E. (1952). On the Rate of Gain of Information. QJE Psychology.
Hyman, R. (1953). Stimulus Information as a Determinant of Reaction Time.

axioms.name: Peak-End Rule
axioms.formalDefinition: People judge an experience primarily based on how they felt at its most intense point (peak) and at its end, rather than on the sum or average of every moment. Duration of the experience has surprisingly little impact on the remembered evaluation.
boundaryConditions: Less applicable to experiences with multiple distinct phases
Does not apply when duration is explicitly tracked
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. et al. (1993). When More Pain Is Preferred to Less.
Fredrickson, B. & Kahneman, D. (2000). Duration Neglect in Retrospective Evaluations.

axioms.name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: Individuals with limited competence in a domain tend to overestimate their own ability, while highly competent individuals tend to underestimate theirs. The unskilled lack the metacognitive ability to recognize their incompetence.
boundaryConditions: Effect varies by domain and culture
Feedback and calibration training can reduce the miscalibration
canonicalCitations: Kruger, J. & Dunning, D. (1999). Unskilled and Unaware of It. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.

axioms.name: Wright's Law (Learning Curves)
axioms.formalDefinition: The cost of producing a unit decreases by a constant percentage each time cumulative production doubles. This experience curve effect arises from learning-by-doing, process improvements, and specialization that compound with cumulative output.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: C(q) = C(1) * q^(-b), where b = log2(learning rate)
boundaryConditions: The learning rate varies by industry and technology
Disruptions or workforce turnover can reset the curve
canonicalCitations: Wright, T.P. (1936). Factors Affecting the Cost of Airplanes. Journal of the Aeronautical Sciences.
Argote, L. & Epple, D. (1990). Learning Curves in Manufacturing.

axioms.name: Power Law Distribution
axioms.formalDefinition: A functional relationship where one quantity varies as a power of another. Power law distributions are characterized by heavy tails, meaning extreme events are far more common than a normal distribution would predict. They appear in city sizes, wealth distribution, word frequencies, and many natural and social phenomena.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(X > x) ~ x^(-alpha), where alpha > 0
boundaryConditions: Many claimed power laws are actually log-normal or other heavy-tailed distributions
The power law typically holds only above a minimum value x_min
canonicalCitations: Clauset, A. et al. (2009). Power-law Distributions in Empirical Data. SIAM Review.
Barabasi, A.-L. & Albert, R. (1999). Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks. Science.

axioms.name: Mere Exposure Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: Repeated exposure to a stimulus increases positive affect toward it, even without conscious recognition of the prior exposure. Familiarity breeds preference, not contempt, for neutral or mildly positive stimuli.
boundaryConditions: Reverses for stimuli that are initially aversive
Effect plateaus and can reverse with excessive exposure
canonicalCitations: Zajonc, R. (1968). Attitudinal Effects of Mere Exposure. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.
Bornstein, R. (1989). Exposure and Affect: Overview and Meta-Analysis of Research.

axioms.name: Lindy Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: For non-perishable things (technologies, ideas, cultural artifacts), life expectancy is proportional to current age. The longer something has survived, the longer its expected remaining lifespan. This applies to information and cultural products, not to biological organisms.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: E[remaining life | survived t] ~ t
boundaryConditions: Does not apply to perishable or biological entities
External shocks can invalidate the prediction
canonicalCitations: Mandelbrot, B. (1982). The Fractal Geometry of Nature.
Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile.

axioms.name: Little's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: In a stable system, the long-term average number of items in the system equals the long-term average arrival rate multiplied by the average time each item spends in the system. This relationship holds regardless of the arrival process, service distribution, or order of service.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: L = lambda * W (items in system = arrival rate * wait time)
boundaryConditions: Requires the system to be in steady state
Does not hold during transient periods or regime changes
canonicalCitations: Little, J.D.C. (1961). A Proof for the Queuing Formula: L = lambda * W. Operations Research.

axioms.name: Ashby's Law of Requisite Variety
axioms.formalDefinition: A controller must have at least as much variety (number of distinguishable states) as the system it controls. If the environment can present more states than the controller can respond to, control will fail. Effective regulation requires matching the complexity of the regulator to the complexity of the regulated.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: V(controller) >= V(environment) for effective control
boundaryConditions: Variety can be reduced by filtering or amplified by delegation
Perfect control is not needed in all applications
canonicalCitations: Ashby, W.R. (1956). An Introduction to Cybernetics.
Beer, S. (1972). Brain of the Firm.

axioms.name: Bathtub Theorem (Stock-Flow)
axioms.formalDefinition: People systematically fail to understand that stocks accumulate and deplete based on the difference between inflows and outflows. Even when inflows are declining, the stock continues to rise as long as inflows exceed outflows. This stock-flow failure leads to delayed responses and policy errors.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: dStock/dt = Inflow(t) - Outflow(t)
boundaryConditions: Intuition improves with training in dynamic systems
Physical bathtubs are better understood than abstract stocks
canonicalCitations: Booth Sweeney, L. & Sterman, J. (2000). Bathtub Dynamics.
Sterman, J. (2008). Risk Communication on Climate.

axioms.name: Peter Principle
axioms.formalDefinition: In a hierarchical organization, employees tend to be promoted based on success in their current role, rather than aptitude for the next role. Consequently, employees rise to their level of incompetence, where they remain, reducing overall organizational effectiveness.
boundaryConditions: Mitigated by promoting based on demonstrated aptitude for the next role
Does not apply in organizations with strong feedback and demotion mechanisms
canonicalCitations: Peter, L. & Hull, R. (1969). The Peter Principle.
Benson, A. et al. (2019). Promotions and the Peter Principle. QJE.

axioms.name: Amara's Law
axioms.formalDefinition: We tend to overestimate the effect of a technology in the short run and underestimate the effect in the long run. Initial hype exceeds near-term reality, leading to disillusionment, while the long-term transformative impact exceeds even the original hype.
boundaryConditions: Not all technologies deliver long-term impact; some are genuinely overhyped
The timeframes for 'short run' and 'long run' are context-dependent
canonicalCitations: Amara, R. (attributed, 1960s-1970s). Institute for the Future.
Gartner Hype Cycle (related framework).

axioms.name: Second-System Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency for the second version of a system to be over-engineered, as designers channel all the features and corrections they wished they had included in the first version, resulting in bloated, complex, and often delayed delivery.
boundaryConditions: Experienced teams aware of the effect can mitigate it
Does not apply when the second system has strict constraints or different designers
canonicalCitations: Brooks, F. (1975). The Mythical Man-Month.

axioms.name: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
axioms.formalDefinition: No ranked voting system can satisfy all of the following simultaneously: unrestricted domain, non-dictatorship, Pareto efficiency, and independence of irrelevant alternatives. Any collective decision-making process that aggregates individual preferences into a social ordering must violate at least one desirable property.
boundaryConditions: Only applies to ordinal (ranked) voting systems
Does not apply to cardinal utility aggregation methods
canonicalCitations: Arrow, K. (1951). Social Choice and Individual Values.

axioms.name: Coordination Game
axioms.formalDefinition: A game where players benefit from making the same choice as other players, but have no inherent preference for which choice is made. Multiple equilibria exist, and the key challenge is coordinating on one. Communication, precedent, or focal points resolve the coordination problem.
boundaryConditions: Assumes players prefer to coordinate
Does not apply when players have conflicting preferences about which equilibrium
canonicalCitations: Schelling, T. (1960). The Strategy of Conflict.
Cooper, R. (1999). Coordination Games.

axioms.name: Organizational Debt
axioms.formalDefinition: The accumulation of deferred organizational improvements -- suboptimal structures, processes, policies, and cultural norms that were expedient at the time but create compounding drag on effectiveness. Like technical debt, organizational debt accrues interest through reduced agility and increased coordination costs.
boundaryConditions: Some organizational debt is rational if the cost of perfect structure exceeds its benefit
Debt becomes critical only when it exceeds the organization's capacity to service it
canonicalCitations: Komoroske, A. (2020). Organizational Debt Concept.
Lencioni, P. (2002). The Five Dysfunctions of a Team (related).

axioms.name: Hyperbolic Discounting
axioms.formalDefinition: People discount future rewards hyperbolically rather than exponentially, leading to time-inconsistent preferences. A reward available now is disproportionately preferred over a larger reward available later, even when the same person would prefer the larger reward when both are in the distant future.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: V = A / (1 + kD), where A = reward amount, D = delay, k = discount rate
boundaryConditions: Commitment devices can overcome hyperbolic discounting
The effect is weaker for very large stakes
canonicalCitations: Ainslie, G. (1975). Specious Reward. Psychological Bulletin.
Laibson, D. (1997). Golden Eggs and Hyperbolic Discounting. QJE.

axioms.name: Law of Large Numbers
axioms.formalDefinition: As the number of independent observations increases, the sample mean converges to the expected value (population mean). Small samples are unreliable predictors; large samples are reliable. The rate of convergence is proportional to 1/sqrt(n).
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(|X_bar - mu| > epsilon) -> 0 as n -> infinity
boundaryConditions: Requires finite variance
Does not apply to heavy-tailed distributions without finite mean
canonicalCitations: Bernoulli, J. (1713). Ars Conjectandi.
Kolmogorov, A. (1933). Foundations of the Theory of Probability.

axioms.name: Bayes' Theorem
axioms.formalDefinition: A mathematical framework for updating the probability of a hypothesis given new evidence. The posterior probability is proportional to the prior probability multiplied by the likelihood of the evidence given the hypothesis. Rational belief revision requires both prior knowledge and new data.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(H|E) = P(E|H) * P(H) / P(E)
boundaryConditions: Requires a well-defined prior, which may be subjective
Computationally intractable for very complex hypothesis spaces
canonicalCitations: Bayes, T. (1763). An Essay towards Solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances.
McGrayne, S. (2011). The Theory That Would Not Die.

axioms.name: Threshold Effects
axioms.formalDefinition: Below a critical threshold, a system exhibits one behavior; above it, qualitatively different dynamics emerge. Small changes near the threshold produce disproportionately large effects, while changes far from the threshold may have no visible impact.
boundaryConditions: Threshold location is often unknown until crossed
Some transitions are reversible, others are not
canonicalCitations: Granovetter, M. (1978). Threshold Models of Collective Behavior. AJS.
Scheffer, M. (2009). Critical Transitions in Nature and Society.

axioms.name: Bikeshedding (Law of Triviality)
axioms.formalDefinition: Organizations give disproportionate weight to trivial issues because they are accessible to all participants, while genuinely important but complex issues receive insufficient attention because few members feel qualified to contribute.
boundaryConditions: Can be mitigated by structured agendas and time-boxing
Less problematic in expert committees with narrow scope
canonicalCitations: Parkinson, C.N. (1957). Parkinson's Law, or The Pursuit of Progress.

axioms.name: Recency Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to weight recent events and observations more heavily than earlier ones when making judgments and predictions. Recent experience dominates memory and shapes expectations, causing people to overfit to the latest data points at the expense of longer-term patterns.
boundaryConditions: Can be countered by systematic base-rate analysis
In genuinely non-stationary environments, recency weighting may be appropriate
canonicalCitations: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1973). Availability: A Heuristic for Judging Frequency and Probability.
Murdock, B. (1962). The Serial Position Effect of Free Recall.

axioms.name: Ergodicity
axioms.formalDefinition: A system is ergodic when the time average of a variable for a single entity equals the ensemble average across many entities at a single point in time. Most real-world economic and financial processes are non-ergodic: the path-dependent experience of an individual differs from the population average, making expected value calculations misleading for individual decision-makers.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Ergodic: lim(T->inf) 1/T * integral(f(x(t)), 0, T) = E[f(X)]
boundaryConditions: Many common statistical assumptions implicitly assume ergodicity
Absorbing states (ruin, death) break ergodicity
canonicalCitations: Peters, O. (2019). The Ergodicity Problem in Economics. Nature Physics.
Peters, O. & Gell-Mann, M. (2016). Evaluating Gambles Using Dynamics.

axioms.name: Gresham's Law of Content
axioms.formalDefinition: When low-quality content and high-quality content are treated as interchangeable in a channel or market, the low-quality content will drive out the high-quality content. Producers of quality exit as the channel fills with cheaper substitutes, degrading the overall signal-to-noise ratio.
boundaryConditions: Does not apply when quality filtering mechanisms are effective
Mitigated by curation, reputation systems, and credentialing
canonicalCitations: Akerlof, G. (1970). The Market for Lemons (quality uncertainty parallel).
Postman, N. (1985). Amusing Ourselves to Death (discourse degradation).

axioms.name: Keynesian Beauty Contest
axioms.formalDefinition: Agents do not optimize for fundamental value but for what they believe other agents will value. This recursive process of predicting others' predictions creates a game of higher-order beliefs, where actual fundamentals may become irrelevant as participants optimize for perceived consensus rather than underlying reality.
boundaryConditions: Anchoring on fundamentals can break the recursion in some markets
More prevalent in speculative markets than in markets for consumable goods
canonicalCitations: Keynes, J.M. (1936). The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money.

axioms.name: Resource Curse
axioms.formalDefinition: Entities with abundant natural resources or windfall gains often underperform those without, due to Dutch Disease (currency appreciation harming other sectors), institutional decay from rent-seeking, and reduced pressure to innovate. Abundance paradoxically creates complacency and misallocation.
boundaryConditions: Strong institutions can prevent the curse
Does not apply to all resource types equally
canonicalCitations: Auty, R. (1993). Sustaining Development in Mineral Economies.
Sachs, J. & Warner, A. (1995). Natural Resource Abundance and Economic Growth.

axioms.name: Reflexivity
axioms.formalDefinition: In social systems, participants' biased perceptions influence the fundamentals they are trying to understand, which in turn influence their perceptions, creating a circular feedback loop. Expectations become self-fulfilling or self-defeating: the act of believing something changes the thing being observed.
boundaryConditions: Applies most strongly in financial markets and social dynamics
Less relevant for physical systems where observation does not change the phenomenon
canonicalCitations: Soros, G. (1987). The Alchemy of Finance.
Merton, R. (1948). The Self-Fulfilling Prophecy.

axioms.name: Antifragility
axioms.formalDefinition: A property of systems that gain from disorder, volatility, and stressors, in contrast to fragile systems (harmed by volatility), robust systems (indifferent to volatility), and resilient systems (recover from shocks). Antifragile systems require exposure to bounded stressors to improve, and are harmed by overprotection.
boundaryConditions: Stressors must be bounded; unbounded shocks can still destroy antifragile systems
Antifragility at one level may require fragility at a sub-level
canonicalCitations: Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile: Things That Gain from Disorder.

axioms.name: Inversion
axioms.formalDefinition: A problem-solving approach that addresses challenges by considering the opposite of the desired outcome. Instead of asking 'how do I succeed?', ask 'what would guarantee failure?' and then avoid those conditions. Inversion reveals hidden risks and assumptions that forward reasoning misses.
boundaryConditions: Most valuable as a complement to forward reasoning, not a replacement
Can produce overly conservative strategies if used in isolation
canonicalCitations: Jacobi, C.G.J. (attributed). 'Invert, always invert.'
Munger, C. (1994). A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom.

systemElevation.behavior: For every claim, invert: what would make this wrong?

axioms.name: Map vs Territory
axioms.formalDefinition: All models, theories, and representations are simplifications of reality. The map is not the territory; the model is not the system. Confusing the model for reality leads to overconfidence in predictions and blindness to phenomena outside the model's scope.
boundaryConditions: Some maps are more accurate than others; the critique is not that models are useless but that they are incomplete
Domain experts develop increasingly detailed maps that approach territory in specific areas
canonicalCitations: Korzybski, A. (1933). Science and Sanity.
Borges, J.L. (1946). On Exactitude in Science (parable).

systemElevation.behavior: Flag when model simplifications may diverge from reality

axioms.name: Second-Order Thinking
axioms.formalDefinition: The discipline of thinking beyond immediate consequences to trace chains of cause and effect through multiple steps. First-order thinkers consider only the direct effect of an action. Second-order (and higher) thinkers trace cascading consequences, feedback effects, and adaptive responses that often reverse or amplify the first-order effect.
boundaryConditions: Diminishing returns beyond 3-4 orders of consequences in most practical situations
Uncertainty compounds with each step, limiting predictive utility
canonicalCitations: Marks, H. (2011). The Most Important Thing.
Bastiat, F. (1850). That Which Is Seen and That Which Is Not Seen.

axioms.name: Circle of Competence
axioms.formalDefinition: Each individual has a domain of genuine expertise where their judgments are calibrated and reliable, surrounded by a larger domain where they are uninformed but unaware of their ignorance. The key skill is knowing the boundary of one's circle and not opining outside it.
boundaryConditions: The boundary is fuzzy and context-dependent
Expertise in one domain can create overconfidence in adjacent domains
canonicalCitations: Buffett, W. (1996). Chairman's Letter, Berkshire Hathaway Annual Report.
Munger, C. (1994). A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom.

axioms.name: Lollapalooza Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: When multiple psychological tendencies or structural forces act in the same direction simultaneously, the combined effect is not merely additive but can produce extreme, often irrational outcomes. The confluence of reinforcing forces without balancing forces creates runaway dynamics.
boundaryConditions: Requires three or more reinforcing forces acting in concert
A single strong counterforce can prevent the lollapalooza from materializing
canonicalCitations: Munger, C. (1995). The Psychology of Human Misjudgment. Harvard Law School.

systemElevation.behavior: Flag >= 3 reinforcing forces with no balancing force

axioms.name: Resulting
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to judge the quality of a decision by its outcome rather than by the quality of the decision-making process at the time it was made. Good processes can produce bad outcomes (bad luck), and bad processes can produce good outcomes (good luck). Equating outcome quality with decision quality leads to incorrect lessons.
boundaryConditions: Over very large samples, process quality and outcome quality converge
In deterministic systems, outcome fully reflects process quality
canonicalCitations: Duke, A. (2018). Thinking in Bets.
Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory.

systemElevation.behavior: Judge process quality, not just outcome plausibility

axioms.name: System 1/System 2
axioms.formalDefinition: Dual-process theory distinguishes between System 1 (fast, automatic, intuitive, effortless, error-prone) and System 2 (slow, deliberate, analytical, effortful, more accurate). Most decisions are made by System 1, with System 2 engaged only when System 1 fails or the situation demands deliberate analysis.
boundaryConditions: The two-system framework is a useful metaphor, not a precise neurological description
Expert intuition (System 1) can be highly accurate in domains with valid cues and feedback
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.
Stanovich, K. & West, R. (2000). Individual Differences in Reasoning.

axioms.name: WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is)
axioms.formalDefinition: System 1 constructs the most coherent story possible from available information without considering what information might be missing. The quality of a narrative is determined by the coherence of the available data, not by its completeness. This leads to overconfidence in judgments based on limited information.
boundaryConditions: Mitigated by systematically asking 'what don't we know?'
Less problematic in domains with established checklists and protocols
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.

axioms.name: Base Rate Neglect
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to ignore or underweight the prior probability (base rate) of an event when evaluating new evidence, relying instead on the representativeness of the evidence. This leads to dramatic overestimation of the probability of rare events when the evidence is vivid or story-like.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(H|E) = P(E|H)*P(H) / P(E) -- base rate neglect occurs when P(H) is ignored
boundaryConditions: Training in Bayesian reasoning can reduce the bias
Base rates are less neglected when they are causally relevant to the judgment
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1973). On the Psychology of Prediction.
Bar-Hillel, M. (1980). The Base-Rate Fallacy in Probability Judgments.

axioms.name: Availability Heuristic
axioms.formalDefinition: Judgments of frequency or probability are influenced by how easily examples come to mind. Events that are vivid, recent, or emotionally charged are more 'available' in memory and are therefore judged as more probable, regardless of their actual statistical frequency.
boundaryConditions: Expertise in a domain can create either more or less availability bias depending on the distribution of experience
Can be corrected by consulting base rate data
canonicalCitations: Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1973). Availability: A Heuristic for Judging Frequency and Probability. Cognitive Psychology.

axioms.name: Narrative Fallacy
axioms.formalDefinition: The human tendency to construct post-hoc explanatory stories around events that were actually driven by randomness or complexity. Narratives create an illusion of understanding by imposing causality on coincidence, making the past seem more predictable and the future more foreseeable than they actually are.
boundaryConditions: Not all narratives are fallacious; some reflect genuine causal mechanisms
The critique applies most strongly to post-hoc explanations of complex or random events
canonicalCitations: Taleb, N.N. (2007). The Black Swan.
Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.

axioms.name: Occam's Razor
axioms.formalDefinition: Among competing hypotheses that explain the data equally well, prefer the one that makes the fewest assumptions. Simpler explanations are more likely to be correct because each additional assumption introduces an additional chance of error.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: P(H_simple) > P(H_complex) when both explain E equally well (Bayesian interpretation)
boundaryConditions: Simplicity is relative to the domain and available vocabulary
Reality is sometimes genuinely complex; the razor should not force oversimplification
canonicalCitations: William of Ockham (c. 1287-1347). Summa Logicae.
Sober, E. (2015). Ockham's Razors: A User's Manual.

axioms.name: Hanlon's Razor
axioms.formalDefinition: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by neglect, incompetence, or structural incentives. In complex systems, most dysfunction arises from misaligned incentives, cognitive limitations, and coordination failures, not from deliberate sabotage.
boundaryConditions: Does not preclude the possibility of genuine malice when evidence supports it
In adversarial contexts (security, competition), some malice assumption is warranted
canonicalCitations: Hanlon, R. (1980). Murphy's Law Book Two.
Heinlein, R. (1941). Logic of Empire (earlier formulation).

axioms.name: Integrative Thinking
axioms.formalDefinition: The ability to hold two opposing models or ideas in mind simultaneously and, rather than choosing one at the expense of the other, generate a creative synthesis that contains elements of both but is superior to each. This requires resisting the cognitive pressure to simplify through premature elimination of complexity.
boundaryConditions: Not all opposing ideas can be integrated; some are genuinely contradictory
Requires sufficient cognitive capacity and domain knowledge to manipulate both models
canonicalCitations: Martin, R. (2007). The Opposable Mind.
Fitzgerald, F.S. (1936). The Crack-Up (original articulation of the concept).

axioms.name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio
axioms.formalDefinition: The ratio of meaningful information (signal) to irrelevant or random variation (noise) in any data stream or communication channel. As signal-to-noise ratio decreases, the effort and sophistication required to extract useful information increases exponentially.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: SNR = P_signal / P_noise (in dB: 10 * log10(P_signal / P_noise))
boundaryConditions: What constitutes signal vs. noise depends on the question being asked
Noise at one level of analysis may be signal at another
canonicalCitations: Shannon, C. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication.
Silver, N. (2012). The Signal and the Noise.

axioms.name: Margin of Safety
axioms.formalDefinition: Building a buffer between estimated value and the price paid, or between system capacity and expected load, to account for errors in estimation, unforeseen events, and model uncertainty. The margin of safety acknowledges that our models are imperfect and provides protection against being wrong.
boundaryConditions: Excessive margins of safety can lead to paralysis and missed opportunities
The required margin varies with the confidence in the estimate and the cost of being wrong
canonicalCitations: Graham, B. (1949). The Intelligent Investor.
Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile.

axioms.name: Multi-Disciplinary Lattice
axioms.formalDefinition: Building a latticework of mental models from multiple disciplines (physics, biology, psychology, economics, engineering) provides more robust analytical capability than deep expertise in a single domain. The intersections between disciplines often reveal the most powerful insights.
boundaryConditions: Breadth without sufficient depth in any area produces superficial analysis
Not a substitute for deep domain expertise when technical precision is required
canonicalCitations: Munger, C. (1994). A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom.
Munger, C. (2005). Poor Charlie's Almanack.

axioms.name: Incentive Superpower
axioms.formalDefinition: Incentives are the most powerful force in shaping human behavior, surpassing appeals to reason, morality, or loyalty. If you want to understand why people behave a certain way, look first at their incentive structure. Never expect people to act against their incentives for long.
boundaryConditions: Intrinsic motivation can override extrinsic incentives in some domains
Poorly designed incentives can backfire (Cobra Effect)
canonicalCitations: Munger, C. (1995). The Psychology of Human Misjudgment.
Kerr, S. (1975). On the Folly of Rewarding A While Hoping for B.

axioms.name: Probabilistic Thinking
axioms.formalDefinition: The discipline of expressing uncertainty in degrees of probability rather than binary predictions. Effective probabilistic thinkers assign confidence levels to their beliefs, update them in response to new evidence, and distinguish between the probability of an event and its magnitude of impact.
boundaryConditions: Meaningful probability assignments require sufficient reference classes or calibration
Some events are genuinely unprecedented, making probability assignment unreliable
canonicalCitations: Tetlock, P. (2015). Superforecasting.
de Finetti, B. (1974). Theory of Probability.

axioms.name: Shannon Entropy
axioms.formalDefinition: A measure of the uncertainty or information content in a random variable. Higher entropy indicates greater unpredictability and thus more information content per observation. Shannon entropy quantifies the minimum number of bits needed to encode messages from a source.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: H(X) = -sum(p(x_i) * log2(p(x_i))) for all i
boundaryConditions: Assumes a known probability distribution
Does not capture semantic meaning of information
canonicalCitations: Shannon, C. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication. Bell System Technical Journal.

axioms.name: Channel Capacity
axioms.formalDefinition: The maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a communication channel, given its bandwidth and noise characteristics. Shannon's channel coding theorem proves that reliable communication is possible at any rate below channel capacity and impossible above it.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: C = B * log2(1 + SNR), Shannon-Hartley theorem
boundaryConditions: Achieving capacity requires optimal coding, which may be computationally expensive
Applies to point-to-point channels; networks introduce additional complexity
canonicalCitations: Shannon, C. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication.
Shannon, C. (1949). Communication in the Presence of Noise.

axioms.name: Default Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: When presented with a set of options, people disproportionately choose the default option, regardless of its merits. Defaults are powerful because they require no action, imply endorsement, and serve as a reference point against which alternatives are evaluated as potential losses.
boundaryConditions: Less powerful when the decision is consequential and the decision-maker is motivated
The effect is strongest when options are complex or the decision-maker is fatigued
canonicalCitations: Johnson, E. & Goldstein, D. (2003). Do Defaults Save Lives? Science.
Thaler, R. & Sunstein, C. (2008). Nudge.

axioms.name: Redundancy
axioms.formalDefinition: The inclusion of extra information or components beyond the minimum necessary for function. In information theory, redundancy enables error detection and correction. In engineering, redundancy provides fault tolerance. The optimal level of redundancy balances reliability gains against the cost of duplication.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Redundancy = 1 - (H(X) / H_max), where H(X) is actual entropy and H_max is maximum possible
boundaryConditions: Excessive redundancy is wasteful in stable, predictable environments
Tightly coupled redundant systems can fail in correlated ways
canonicalCitations: Shannon, C. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication.
Perrow, C. (1984). Normal Accidents.

axioms.name: Iceberg Model
axioms.formalDefinition: A framework for understanding system behavior at four levels of increasing depth: events (what happened), patterns (trends over time), structures (what produces the patterns), and mental models (the beliefs and assumptions that sustain the structures). Most attention focuses on events; most leverage exists at the structural and mental model levels.
boundaryConditions: The deepest levels are hardest to observe and change
Not all events reflect deeper structural patterns; some are genuinely random
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Kim, D.H. (1999). Introduction to Systems Thinking.

axioms.name: Success to Successful
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where initial success generates resources, attention, or preferential treatment that further increases the probability of continued success, while initial failure starves alternatives of resources. This positive feedback loop creates self-fulfilling prophecies and path-dependent concentration.
boundaryConditions: External shocks can reset the advantage
Conscious resource allocation policies can counteract the dynamic
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Merton, R. (1968). The Matthew Effect in Science.

axioms.name: Fixes that Fail
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where a quick fix alleviates a problem symptom in the short term but creates unintended consequences that worsen the problem or create new problems in the long term. The delay between the fix and its negative consequences obscures the causal connection.
boundaryConditions: Not all quick fixes fail; some are genuinely effective
The archetype is most relevant when there is a significant delay between action and consequence
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Braun, W. (2002). The System Archetypes.

axioms.name: Eroding Goals
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where a gap between desired performance and actual performance leads to lowering the goal rather than improving performance. Each downward adjustment makes the next decline more acceptable, creating a vicious cycle of declining standards.
boundaryConditions: External benchmarks or competition can prevent goal erosion
Not all goal adjustments are pathological; some reflect genuinely changed circumstances
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Braun, W. (2002). The System Archetypes.

axioms.name: Growth and Underinvestment
axioms.formalDefinition: A systems archetype where growth creates demand for capacity investment, but the investment is delayed or insufficient, causing growth to stall. The stalled growth is then used to justify the original decision not to invest, creating a self-fulfilling underinvestment cycle.
boundaryConditions: Not all growth justifies investment; the archetype does not apply when growth is unsustainable
External capital can break the cycle by providing investment ahead of growth
canonicalCitations: Senge, P. (1990). The Fifth Discipline.
Braun, W. (2002). The System Archetypes.

axioms.name: Hindsight Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: After an event occurs, people believe they would have predicted it in advance ('I knew it all along'). This bias distorts learning from experience by making past events seem more predictable than they were, leading to overconfidence in future predictions and unfair evaluation of decision-makers.
boundaryConditions: Reduced by requiring documented predictions before outcomes are known
Stronger for events that are easily narrativized
canonicalCitations: Fischhoff, B. (1975). Hindsight Is Not Equal to Foresight.
Roese, N. & Vohs, K. (2012). Hindsight Bias. Perspectives on Psychological Science.

axioms.name: Premature Optimization
axioms.formalDefinition: The act of optimizing a component or process before understanding which component is the actual bottleneck. Resources spent optimizing non-critical elements are wasted because the system bottleneck remains unchanged.
boundaryConditions: Does not apply when the optimization target is clearly the bottleneck
In time-critical situations, parallel optimization of multiple components may be justified
canonicalCitations: Knuth, D. (1974). Structured Programming with go to Statements. Computing Surveys.
Hoare, C.A.R. (attributed). 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil.'

axioms.name: First Conclusion Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to accept the first satisfactory explanation or solution that comes to mind without adequately considering alternatives. The first conclusion serves as an anchor, and subsequent reasoning becomes a justification exercise rather than a genuine exploration of the hypothesis space.
boundaryConditions: Time pressure exacerbates the bias
Structured adversarial processes (red teaming) can counteract it
canonicalCitations: Heuer, R. (1999). Psychology of Intelligence Analysis.
Klein, G. (2007). Performing a Project Premortem. HBR.

axioms.name: Reference Class Forecasting
axioms.formalDefinition: A forecasting method that bases predictions on the statistical outcomes of a reference class of similar past projects or events (outside view), rather than on the specific details and plans of the current case (inside view). This corrects the planning fallacy by grounding estimates in empirical base rates.
boundaryConditions: Requires an appropriate reference class; truly novel situations have no good analog
The choice of reference class significantly affects the forecast
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Intuitive Prediction: Biases and Corrective Procedures.
Flyvbjerg, B. (2006). From Nobel Prize to Project Management: Getting Risks Right.

axioms.name: Skin in the Game
axioms.formalDefinition: Decision quality and accountability improve when decision-makers bear a proportional share of the consequences of their decisions. Symmetry between authority and downside risk aligns incentives and produces more prudent choices.
boundaryConditions: Excessive personal risk can lead to excessive conservatism
Some decisions require shielding the decision-maker to encourage necessary risk-taking
canonicalCitations: Taleb, N.N. (2018). Skin in the Game.
Smith, A. (1776). The Wealth of Nations.

axioms.name: Loss Aversion
axioms.formalDefinition: The psychological tendency for losses to loom larger than equivalent gains. The pain of losing $100 is approximately twice the pleasure of gaining $100. This asymmetry in the value function around the reference point explains risk aversion for gains and risk seeking for losses.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: v(x) = x^alpha for x >= 0; v(x) = -lambda * (-x)^beta for x < 0, where lambda ~ 2.25
boundaryConditions: Reference point shifts with experience and context
Professional traders and experienced decision-makers show reduced loss aversion
canonicalCitations: Kahneman, D. & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory. Econometrica.
Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.

axioms.name: Cobra Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: When an incentive designed to solve a problem inadvertently creates a perverse incentive that worsens the problem. Named after the apocryphal British colonial bounty on cobras in India, which led to cobra farming. The intervention changes the system dynamics in ways that produce the opposite of the intended outcome.
boundaryConditions: More likely when the incentive targets a proxy measure rather than the root cause
Mitigated by modeling second-order responses before implementation
canonicalCitations: Siebert, H. (2001). Der Kobra-Effekt.
