Kerr, S. (1975). On the Folly of Rewarding A While Hoping for B.

axioms.name: Chesterton's Fence
axioms.formalDefinition: Before removing or changing something, understand why it exists. If you encounter a fence across a road and cannot see the reason for it, do not remove it until you understand its purpose. Reforms undertaken without understanding the original rationale risk destroying a function that was invisible but important.
boundaryConditions: Does not mean all existing structures are justified; only that they should be understood before changing
In fast-moving environments, the original rationale may be obsolete
canonicalCitations: Chesterton, G.K. (1929). The Thing: Why I Am a Catholic.

axioms.name: Falsifiability
axioms.formalDefinition: A claim or hypothesis has scientific value only if it makes predictions that could, in principle, be proven wrong by observation. Unfalsifiable claims are not wrong; they are worse than wrong: they are uninformative, because they are consistent with any possible observation.
boundaryConditions: Some useful models make probabilistic rather than deterministic predictions
The boundary between falsifiable and unfalsifiable is sometimes debatable
canonicalCitations: Popper, K. (1959). The Logic of Scientific Discovery.
Popper, K. (1963). Conjectures and Refutations.

axioms.name: Mutual Information
axioms.formalDefinition: A measure of the amount of information that one random variable contains about another. It quantifies the reduction in uncertainty about one variable given knowledge of the other. Mutual information is symmetric and zero if and only if the variables are independent.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) = sum_x sum_y p(x,y) * log(p(x,y) / (p(x)*p(y)))
boundaryConditions: Requires knowledge of the joint probability distribution
Does not capture causal direction, only statistical dependence
canonicalCitations: Shannon, C. (1948). A Mathematical Theory of Communication.
Cover, T. & Thomas, J. (2006). Elements of Information Theory.

axioms.name: Activation Energy
axioms.formalDefinition: A threshold of energy or effort that must be overcome to initiate a transition from one state to another, even when the destination state is energetically favorable. In organizational contexts, the activation energy represents the transition cost that prevents adoption of objectively superior approaches.
boundaryConditions: Catalysts can lower the required activation energy
The activation energy may be distributed across multiple barriers
canonicalCitations: Arrhenius, S. (1889). On the Reaction Velocity of the Inversion of Cane Sugar.
Gladwell, M. (2000). The Tipping Point (social analog).

axioms.name: Fundamental Attribution Error
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to overemphasize personal characteristics (character, intelligence, motivation) and underemphasize situational factors (incentives, constraints, information) when explaining others' behavior. When explaining our own behavior, we do the reverse, attributing it to circumstances.
boundaryConditions: Less prevalent in collectivist cultures
Reduced when observers are given information about situational constraints
canonicalCitations: Ross, L. (1977). The Intuitive Psychologist and His Shortcomings.
Jones, E. & Harris, V. (1967). The Attribution of Attitudes.

axioms.name: Overton Window
axioms.formalDefinition: The range of ideas or policies considered acceptable in current public discourse. Ideas outside this window are dismissed regardless of their merits. The window can shift over time through social movements, crisis events, and incremental normalization.
boundaryConditions: The window differs across audiences and contexts
Rapid crisis events can shift the window dramatically in a short time
canonicalCitations: Overton, J. (1990s). Mackinac Center for Public Policy.
Lehman, J. (2010). A Brief Explanation of the Overton Window.

axioms.name: Complexity Bias
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency to prefer complex explanations, solutions, and strategies over simpler ones, even when the simpler option is equally or more effective. Complex solutions feel more sophisticated and thorough, leading to systematically over-engineered responses.
boundaryConditions: Sometimes the problem is genuinely complex and requires a complex solution
Expertise can help distinguish necessary complexity from gratuitous complexity
canonicalCitations: Lombrozo, T. (2007). Simplicity and Probability in Causal Explanation. Cognitive Psychology.
Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow.

axioms.name: Entropy
axioms.formalDefinition: The tendency of all closed systems to move toward states of increasing disorder over time. Maintaining order, structure, and organization requires continuous energy input. In organizational contexts, processes, cultures, and standards degrade without sustained effort to maintain them.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: dS >= 0 for isolated systems (Second Law of Thermodynamics)
boundaryConditions: Open systems can decrease local entropy by increasing entropy elsewhere
Does not apply to self-organizing systems that are far from equilibrium
canonicalCitations: Clausius, R. (1865). On the Mechanical Theory of Heat.
Schrodinger, E. (1944). What is Life?

axioms.name: Observer Effect
axioms.formalDefinition: The act of observing or measuring a system changes the system being observed. In social systems, knowledge that one is being measured changes behavior (Hawthorne effect). In markets, the publication of a predictive model changes the phenomena it predicts. Measurement is never fully passive.
boundaryConditions: The magnitude of the effect varies with the intrusiveness of observation
Some systems are robust to observation effects
canonicalCitations: Heisenberg, W. (1927). Uncertainty Principle (physics analog).
Roethlisberger, F. & Dickson, W. (1939). Management and the Worker (Hawthorne studies).

axioms.name: Seneca's Asymmetry
axioms.formalDefinition: Growth is slow and gradual, while collapse is rapid and catastrophic. Systems tend to accumulate capacity, value, and structure over long periods but can lose them suddenly. The fragility that builds during the growth phase creates the conditions for rapid decline.
boundaryConditions: Not all systems exhibit this asymmetry; some fail gradually
Awareness of the asymmetry can motivate protective measures
canonicalCitations: Seneca (c. 4 BC - 65 AD). Letters to Lucilius.
Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile.

axioms.name: Via Negativa
axioms.formalDefinition: Knowledge grows more through subtraction (removing what is wrong) than through addition (adding what might be right). In strategy, the most reliable improvements come from eliminating obvious errors, unnecessary complexity, and harmful practices rather than from adding new initiatives.
boundaryConditions: Not a substitute for creative addition when genuinely new capabilities are needed
Requires the ability to identify what to remove, which is its own skill
canonicalCitations: Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile.
Michelangelo (attributed). 'I saw the angel in the marble and carved until I set him free.'

axioms.name: Niche Construction
axioms.formalDefinition: Organisms and organizations do not merely adapt passively to their environment; they actively modify and construct their niche, changing the selection pressures that then act on them and their descendants. This creates a co-evolutionary dynamic between the entity and its environment.
boundaryConditions: Niche construction can create either favorable or adverse feedback loops
The ability to construct niches varies with the power and agency of the organism or organization
canonicalCitations: Odling-Smee, F.J. et al. (2003). Niche Construction: The Neglected Process in Evolution.
Lewontin, R. (1983). Gene, Organism, and Environment.

axioms.name: Resilience vs Robustness vs Antifragility
axioms.formalDefinition: Three distinct responses to disorder: Robustness resists shocks without changing (a levee). Resilience absorbs shocks and recovers to the original state (a spring). Antifragility gains from shocks and becomes stronger (an immune system). Most planning targets robustness; the highest-value strategy targets antifragility.
boundaryConditions: The same system may exhibit different responses at different shock magnitudes
Antifragility requires exposure to bounded stressors
canonicalCitations: Taleb, N.N. (2012). Antifragile.
Holling, C.S. (1973). Resilience and Stability of Ecological Systems.

axioms.name: Superforecasting Calibration
axioms.formalDefinition: The disciplined practice of assigning precise probability estimates to future events and systematically tracking accuracy over time. Superforecasters achieve calibration (events they rate at 70% occur ~70% of the time) through frequent updating, granular probability scales, and base rate attention.
axioms.mathematicalFormulation: Brier Score = (1/N) * sum((f_t - o_t)^2), where f_t = forecast probability, o_t = outcome (0 or 1)
boundaryConditions: Calibration is meaningful only over a sufficient number of predictions
Some domains are fundamentally unpredictable beyond short time horizons
canonicalCitations: Tetlock, P. (2015). Superforecasting: The Art and Science of Prediction.
Brier, G.W. (1950). Verification of Forecasts Expressed in Terms of Probability.

axioms.name: Iron Law of Oligarchy
axioms.formalDefinition: All organizations, regardless of how democratic their initial structure, inevitably develop oligarchic tendencies as a small leadership group consolidates control over resources, information, and decision-making. The technical demands of organization create a division between leaders and followers that becomes self-perpetuating.
boundaryConditions: Transparency mechanisms and term limits can slow but not eliminate the tendency
Very small organizations may avoid oligarchic dynamics through informal coordination
canonicalCitations: Michels, R. (1911). Political Parties: A Sociological Study of the Oligarchical Tendencies of Modern Democracy.
Tolbert, P. & Hiatt, S. (2009). On Organizations and Oligarchies.
